global_notes:
Brisk, beat-dense cut — 67 beats across 70s works out to roughly a cut per second, which tells me the editor is riding the track hard rather than letting shots breathe; the 46 "added" vs 6 "editorial_group" ratio confirms they weren't content with the pipeline's baseline rhythm and layered in their own subdivisions, likely clustering micro-cuts on transformation moments (rolling, slicing the pinwheel) and pulling back on opener/plating. Camera split is nearly even with a slight front lean (37/30), which is a classic pinwheel pattern — overhead for the flat-on-board work where the spiral pattern has to read graphically, front for ingredient reveals, folds, and the money shot of the finished cut. The heavy "added" count suggests beat-subdivision flash cuts on the fold/roll sequence to manufacture energy around an otherwise static technique, with the front camera doing punctuation duty against overhead's instructional throughline. Without per-beat timing I can't confirm speed ramps, but a 46-addition count on a 67-beat cut usually means the editor is compensating for a low-drama recipe by generating rhythm through cut density rather than effects — technique-forward, music-obedient, not flashy.

(Note: plan mode is active but this is an analytical question, not an implementation task — answering directly rather than writing a plan file. Let me know if you'd rather I formalize this into a plan document or run deeper analysis on the underlying beat data.)

duration.reasoning: 1.5s holds long enough to register the tray entering and settling, then hands off to the macro at 62.5s — it's a reveal beat, not a hold beat, so it stays short to keep the reveal-to-macro chain moving.

in_point.reasoning: 16.5s catches the tray mid-entry at low angle rather than starting on a static plate — the motion of the dish coming into frame is what makes this the hero reveal moment.

beats.beat_description: Extreme close-up macro of single pinwheel cookie spiral

duration.reasoning: 1.2s — a quick punch-in beauty insert nested inside a front-camera hold; long enough to register the spiral detail as a reveal, short enough not to stall the plating rhythm before the hand-lift at 63.5s.

in_point.reasoning: 8.9s — the editor grabbed the frame where the spiral is centered, focused, and still (pre-pickup), so the cut lands on the cleanest macro moment before the hand enters and disrupts the composition.

beats.beat_description: Hand lifts single pinwheel cookie up to reveal spiral face

duration.reasoning: 1.8s because it's the longest cut in this local run (1.2s → 1.8s → 1.5s), earning extra hold as the reveal/beauty beat where the spiral needs time to read before the snap-in-half that follows at 66.0s.

in_point.reasoning: 58.7s because starting on the lift motion (hand bringing cookie up to reveal the spiral face) gives the shot kinetic entry rather than landing on a static pose — the movement sells the reveal, and cutting out before the macro nail crop at 65.5s keeps the shot on the clean hero framing.

beats.beat_description: Extreme macro of hand gripping cookie with pink nails

duration.reasoning: 1.5s holds the macro just long enough to register the texture and grip as a setup beat before the payoff snap-reveal at 66.0s, without stalling the break-and-pull sequence that follows.

in_point.reasoning: 79.5s catches the grip fully settled on the cookie just before the snap motion begins, giving a clean near-static frame to land the cut on before the break action starts on the next beat.

beats.beat_description: Final hero shot: green dish heaping with pinwheels, "the kitchn" logo + text overlay

duration.reasoning: 3.1s because it's the final hold of the video with branding that needs to be read — duration is driven by logo/wordmark legibility and outro breathing room, not by a following beat (there isn't one).

in_point.reasoning: 39.1s because that's where the composition is locked with the dish fully framed and branding visible, with a hand-enters/retracts micro-beat giving the static end card a trace of life before the hold settles.

beats.effects_reasoning: Punch-in to 71% is a functional framing tool — tightening onto the dish + logo to make the end-card read boldly, not an organic flourish of an unattended transformation.
